hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Use the modulus operator (%) to determine if the number is even or odd.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: If the number % 2 equals 0, it is even; otherwise, it is odd.

segments.title: Intuition
segments.desc: First I remebered that any even integer is divisible by two, but odd integers are one greater than an even integer.

segments.title: Implementation
segments.desc: For this problem I used the modulos operator, Math.abs, and an if statement.

segments.title: Implementation
Analysis
segments.desc: If the integer 10 is inputted, 10 modulos 2 will return 0, the Math.abs does nothing, but 0 is equal to 0 so the method will return even. If the integer is -9, -9 modulos 2 will return -1, the Math.abs makes this integer a positive 1, since 1 is not equal to 0, the if statement is ignored and the method returns Odd.

segments.title: Complexity
segments.desc: This approach has a time complexity of O(1) since the method takes the same amount of operations for any integer.

segments.title: Problem
segments.desc: Write a function that takes an integer as input and returns 'Even' if the number is even, and 'Odd' if the number is odd.

segments.title: Constraint
segments.desc: The input will be a valid integer.

segments.title: Code
Example 1
segments.desc:
Input: 4 
Output: Even

segments.title: Example 2
segments.desc:
Input: 7
Output: Odd

replies.text: Yeah same, once I understood complements it made sense.

comments.text: I first tried brute force but it was too slow. The HashMap solution is way better.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Try checking every pair first

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: Think about using a HashMap to store numnbers you've already seen.

segments.title: Intuition
segments.desc: We want two numbers that add to target. Instead of checking every pair, we can store numbers we’ve already seen.

segments.title: Code
Complexity
segments.desc: Time: O(n), Space: O(n)

segments.title: Problem
segments.desc: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

segments.title: Constraint
segments.desc: 2 <= nums.length <= 10000. Each input has exactly one solution. You may not use the same element twice.

segments.title: Code
Example 1
segments.desc:
Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9
Output: [0,1]
Explanation: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].

segments.title: Example 2
segments.desc:
Input: nums = [3,2,4], target = 6
Output: [1,2]

comments.text: I forgot to ignore punctuation at first and got it wrong.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Ignore non-alphanumeric characters.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: Compare characters from both ends.

segments.title: Code
Problem
segments.desc: Given a string, determine if it is a palindrome, considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring cases.

segments.title: Constraint
segments.desc: The string may include spaces and punctuation.

segments.title: Code
Example 1
segments.desc:
Input: "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama" 
Output: true

segments.title: Example 2
segments.desc:
Input: "race a car" 
Output: false

comments.text: Super straightforward once you think about swapping.

replies.text: Fr once you learn it you start seeing it in everything.

comments.text: Two pointers show up everywhere lol.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Use two pointers.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: Swap elements from the ends moving inward.

segments.title: Code
Problem
segments.desc: Write a function that reverses a string. The input string is given as a character array.

segments.title: Constraint
segments.desc: You must do this in-place with O(1) extra memory.

segments.title: Code

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Try using dynamic programming to count the number of unique BSTs for each number of nodes.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: The number of unique BSTs for n nodes is the sum over all possible roots: left * right, where left and right are the number of unique BSTs in the left and right subtrees.

segments.title: Intuition
segments.desc: This is a classic dynamic programming problem. The number of unique BSTs for n nodes is the sum of the number of unique BSTs for all possible left and right subtree combinations.

segments.title: Code
Complexity
segments.desc: Time: O(n^2), Space: O(n)

segments.title: Intuition
segments.desc: This solution uses the closed-form formula for the nth Catalan number, which counts the number of unique BSTs. It iteratively computes the result using integer arithmetic.

segments.title: Code
Complexity
segments.desc: Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

segments.title: Problem
segments.desc: Given an integer n, return the number of structurally unique BST's (binary search trees) which has exactly n nodes of unique values from 1 to n.

segments.title: Code
Example 1
segments.desc:
Input: n = 3
Output: 5

segments.title: Example 2
segments.desc:
Input: n = 1
Output: 1

segments.title: Description
segments.desc: No description provided.

segments.title: Description
segments.desc: No description provided.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.desc: Try every possible subarray and compute its sum.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.desc: Can you use a HashMap to optimize?

segments.title: Problem Statement
segments.desc:
Given an integer array nums and an integer sum k, return the length of the longest contiguous subarray whose total equals k. The array can contain negative numbers.
Example 1: nums = [1, -1, 5, -2, 3], k = 3 → Output: 4. Example 2: nums = [-2, -1, 2, 1], k = 3 → Output: 2.

segments.title: Follow-up Questions
segments.desc:
- What is the time complexity of your algorithm?
- Can you find a way to make your algorithm faster?

Example 1: nums = [1, -1, 5, -2, 3], k = 3 → Output: 4. Example 2: nums = [-2, -1, 2, 1], k = 3 → Output: 2.

segments.title: Follow-up Questions
segments.desc:
- What is the time complexity of your algorithm?
- Can you find a way to make your algorithm faster?
